lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market insurance agencies and brokerages that procure, place, service, renew, and advise on insurance for external customers on a recurring or repeat basis.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal insurance units, carrier-owned sales forces acting solely for one insurer, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commercial and personal customers receive placement, policy service, renewal, claims assistance, and risk advice; agency revenue is primarily carrier commission, including renewal commission, with some fee-based advice.

a.rationale: The wage-weighted task share is estimated from the industry’s core sales-and-service work. Insurance sales-agent duties include interviewing clients, explaining policies, analyzing current coverage, renewals, claims assistance, and record maintenance; agency AI evidence identifies data entry, comparisons, routine service, document analysis, and administrative workflow as susceptible to AI assistance [S1, S2]. Advisory judgment, relationship building, licensed sales, and complex-risk placement remain outside the exposed share.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS occupation-by-wage matrix combined with time-and-motion samples from agency management systems, plus a task-level study of producer, account-manager, and CSR work, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No public source supplies a NAICS 524210 wage-weighted task inventory.
The occupational source includes sales agents outside agencies and excludes some agency support roles.

rho.rationale: Implementation is bounded below the exposed task share because only 8% of surveyed independent agencies had AI embedded in daily workflows, while 68% planned to increase AI use in the next year [S2]. Data privacy or compliance concerns, inaccurate outputs, disconnected systems, and limited formal policies constrain deployment. The base assumes human-reviewed automation of routine work rather than autonomous insurance advice or binding.
rho.change_evidence: The full Big I longitudinal agency survey with workflow-level adoption, vendor telemetry from agency-management-system integrations, and post-implementation staffing data for LMM agencies would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The Big I survey targets independent-agency members of varying sizes, not the frozen EBITDA band.
NAIC AI guidance addresses insurers and may constrain agencies indirectly through carrier requirements rather than directly.

e.rationale: Most LMM firms classified here are expected to be external-customer agencies or brokerages with recurring renewal and service work. The allowance below one excludes captive or otherwise non-transferable operations mixed into the code. BLS distinguishes captive agents from independent agents and describes brokers as client representatives; this supports, but does not measure, the operational distinction [S1].
e.change_evidence: A firm-level NAICS 524210 business register matched to carrier appointments, ownership records, and revenue composition would materially change this estimate.
caveats: There is no published LMM-band tabulation of captive, independent, fee-only, and non-operating firms within NAICS 524210.
Eligibility is a firm construct, whereas the cited source describes workers and roles.

s5.bridge: Vertafore measures retirement intentions of insurance professionals rather than ownership-control transfers of LMM NAICS 524210 firms: about one quarter expected to retire within five years, and half of retiring owners and principals reported a succession plan [S5]. MarshBerry measures announced US insurance-brokerage transactions across all target sizes: 847 in 2024, with 593 by private-capital-backed buyers [S4]. These indicators are translated into an LMM qualifying-control-transfer probability after excluding employee retirements, non-transferable closures, and reorganizations. Worker retirement likely overstates owner exit, while announced-deal data likely understates local private transfers, so the net error direction is uncertain.
s5.rationale: A meaningful five-year transfer flow is supported by both retirement/succession evidence and sustained brokerage M&A, but neither source gives transfers for the frozen firm band. The base deliberately remains below the broad workforce retirement signal because transfer requires an eligible owner, operating continuity, and a qualifying control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: A paid transaction database matched to target revenue or EBITDA, state producer-agency ownership filings, and a survey of LMM principals’ planned ownership exits would materially change this estimate.
caveats: MarshBerry transaction counts cover all brokerage sizes and announced transactions, not LMM firms only.
The retirement survey is voluntary and includes employees as well as owners.

q.rationale: The base reflects commission- and renewal-led revenue rather than cost-plus billing: BLS identifies commissions as the most common compensation form and states that their amount depends partly on whether business is new or renewal [S1]. This can preserve some labor productivity benefit, but competitive quoting, carrier platform automation, service expectations, and renewal repricing should share part of the gain with customers or carriers.
q.change_evidence: Agency-level commission schedules, client fee agreements, renewal-price histories, carrier compensation grids, and post-automation margin cohorts would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Employee compensation is not the same as firm revenue, so the source supports the revenue-model inference rather than measuring retention.
Retention differs materially by personal, commercial, employee-benefits, and specialty business.

d5.bridge: BLS projects insurance-sales-agent employment to rise 4% from 2024 to 2034 and explains that independent-agent growth should be strongest as carriers rely more on brokerages, while online purchase reduces agent demand [S1]. This occupation-level ten-year nominal headcount outlook is converted to a five-year constant-price, constant-quality quantity outlook for the current agency-service basket. It may understate output growth if technology raises productivity and may overstate demand if headcount is retained despite lower service quantity.
d5.rationale: The central case is near-flat to modestly positive service demand. Continued need for new customers and independent distribution offsets online self-service, but does not justify extrapolating the occupation projection as a direct measure of industry output.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar NAICS 524210 output data, carrier distribution-channel premium and commission data, and a cohort study of policies serviced per agency worker would materially change this estimate.
caveats: BLS projects an occupation, not real output or the specified service basket.
Premium inflation, catastrophe cycles, and rate changes are excluded from this construct but can dominate observed agency revenue.

o.bridge: The Big I evidence comes from independent-agency members rather than every LMM firm: it reports that 85% to 90% of customers still want agent guidance for purchases and policy management, while routine data entry, comparison, service, and administrative work move to AI [S2]. BLS separately reports that online self-service reduces demand but agents remain needed for advice and policy choice [S1]. These findings are translated into the future share of the current service quantity requiring an accountable agency operator; survey respondents may overstate customer preference for agents, making the proxy potentially upward biased.
o.rationale: Accountable operators should remain necessary for complex coverage explanation, client-specific advice, carrier access, licensed activity, renewals, and claims support, even as simple transactions migrate to digital self-service. State producer licensing reinforces that selling, soliciting, or negotiating insurance is regulated activity [S6].
o.change_evidence: Carrier and insurtech channel-level bind and renewal data, customer-choice experiments by line of business, and state enforcement data on digitally assisted sales would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The customer-guidance measure is an industry association survey rather than observed customer behavior.
The share will vary sharply between simple personal lines and complex commercial or specialty placements.

narrative.executive_view: Insurance agencies and brokerages combine recurring renewal work, active acquisition markets, and a sizeable set of automatable service tasks. The opportunity is constrained by regulated advice, relationship-led selling, fragmented systems, and uncertainty about how much savings survive competitive repricing.
narrative.ai_impact: Routine record work, document analysis, policy comparison, customer service, marketing, and administrative workflows are the clearest AI targets [S1, S2]. In the 2026 Big I survey, 8% of agencies had AI embedded in daily workflows, while 68% planned to increase use within a year, indicating both momentum and a large implementation gap [S2].
narrative.value_capture: Commissions, including renewal commissions, are common in agent compensation [S1], supporting retention of some internal productivity gain. But customers seek rapid, transparent digital service and carriers or competitors can capture part of the gain, particularly in standardized policies [S2, S7].
narrative.firm_availability: Brokerage M&A was active: MarshBerry recorded 847 announced US transactions in 2024, 593 involving private-capital-backed buyers [S4]. Retirement and succession evidence supplies an additional transfer signal, although neither source isolates the frozen EBITDA band or distinguishes all qualifying control transfers [S5].
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects insurance-sales-agent employment to grow 4% between 2024 and 2034, expecting strongest growth for independents as carriers rely more on brokerages [S1]. The same source warns that online buying reduces need for agents, so durable demand rests most on advice, complex placement, renewal management, and accountable client service rather than transactional quoting.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: AI accuracy, privacy, compliance, disconnected systems, and weak agency governance are practical adoption risks [S2]. State licensing applies to selling, soliciting, and negotiating insurance [S6], while NAIC guidance emphasizes governance, fairness, accuracy, and documentation for insurer AI systems [S3]. Market data is not cleanly segmented by firm size, line of business, or transfer type.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring service and renewal relationships provide a durable operating base while routine workflow work is amenable to human-supervised AI.
narrative.principal_weakness: The best operational savings may be offset by limited implementation capacity and by pricing or service competition in simpler insurance transactions.

sources.title: Insurance Sales Agents: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Insurance-agent duties, licensing, commission and renewal compensation, agency employment share, online-self-service risk, continuing need for advice, and 2024–2034 employment projection.

sources.title: Two-Thirds of Independent Agents Plan to Increase AI Use This Year
sources.supports: 2026 independent-agency survey results on AI adoption, embedded workflow use, planned adoption, expected productivity, compliance and accuracy concerns, and governance gaps.

sources.title: NAIC Members Approve Model Bulletin on Use of AI by Insurers
sources.supports: AI governance, risk management, fairness, accuracy, documentation, and regulatory expectations applicable to insurer AI systems and third parties acting on their behalf.

sources.title: 2024 M&A Year in Review: Outlook for Insurance Distribution
sources.supports: 2024 announced US insurance-brokerage transaction count, private-capital buyer count and share, target-line composition, and limitations of transaction-market proxies.

sources.title: The Insurance Agency Workforce 2024
sources.supports: Insurance-professional retirement timing, owner and principal succession-plan evidence, and workforce age context.

sources.title: Producer Licensing
sources.supports: State licensing and regulation of individuals and entities that sell, solicit, or negotiate insurance.

sources.title: Insights from the 2026 Independent Agency Growth Study
sources.supports: Independent-agency customer retention, changing expectations for real-time information, transparency, online self-service, and value of agent communication and policy explanation.
